31: 32: 33: 34: 35: --- 36: 37: Chatwoot is the modern, open-source, and self-hosted customer support platform designed to help businesses deliver exceptional customer support experience. Built for scale and flexibility, Chatwoot gives you full control over your customer data while providing powerful tools to manage conversations across channels. 38: 39: ### ✨ Captain – AI Agent for Support 40: 41: Supercharge your support with Captain, Chatwoot’s AI agent. Captain helps automate responses, handle common queries, and reduce agent workload—ensuring customers get instant, accurate answers. With Captain, your team can focus on complex conversations while routine questions are resolved automatically. Read more about Captain [here](https://chwt.app/captain-docs). 42: 43: ### 💬 Omnichannel Support Desk 44: 45: Chatwoot centralizes all customer conversations into one powerful inbox, no matter where your customers reach out from. It supports live chat on your website, email, Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, WhatsApp, Telegram, Line, SMS etc. 46: 47: ### 📚 Help center portal 48: 49: Publish help articles, FAQs, and guides through the built-in Help Center Portal. Enable customers to find answers on their own, reduce repetitive queries, and keep your support team focused on more complex issues. 50: 51: ### 🗂️ Other features 52: 53: #### Collaboration & Productivity 54: 55: - Private Notes and @mentions for internal team discussions. 56: - Labels to organize and categorize conversations. 57: - Keyboard Shortcuts and a Command Bar for quick navigation. 58: - Canned Responses to reply faster to frequently asked questions. 59: - Auto-Assignment to route conversations based on agent availability. 60: - Multi-lingual Support to serve customers in multiple languages. 61: - Custom Views and Filters for better inbox organization. 62: - Business Hours and Auto-Responders to manage response expectations. 63: - Teams and Automation tools for scaling support workflows. 64: - Agent Capacity Management to balance workload across the team. 65: 66: #### Customer Data & Segmentation 67: - Contact Management with profiles and interaction history. 68: - Contact Segments and Notes for targeted communication. 69: - Campaigns to proactively engage customers. 70: - Custom Attributes for storing additional customer data. 71: - Pre-Chat Forms to collect user information before starting conversations. 72: 73: #### Integrations 74: - Slack Integration to manage conversations directly from Slack. 75: - Dialogflow Integration for chatbot automation. 76: - Dashboard Apps to embed internal tools within Chatwoot. 77: - Shopify Integration to view and manage customer orders right within Chatwoot. 78: - Use Google Translate to translate messages from your customers in realtime. 79: - Create and manage Linear tickets within Chatwoot. 80: 81: #### Reports & Insights 82: - Live View of ongoing conversations for real-time monitoring. 83: - Conversation, Agent, Inbox, Label, and Team Reports for operational visibility. 84: - CSAT Reports to measure customer satisfaction. 85: - Downloadable Reports for offline analysis and reporting. 86: 87: 88: ## Documentation 89: 90: Detailed documentation is available at [chatwoot.com/help-center](https://www.chatwoot.com/help-center). 91: 92: ## Translation process 93: 94: The translation process for Chatwoot web and mobile app is managed at [https://translate.chatwoot.com](https://translate.chatwoot.com) using Crowdin. Please read the [translation guide](https://www.chatwoot.com/docs/contributing/translating-chatwoot-to-your-language) for contributing to Chatwoot. 95: 96: ## Branching model 97: 98: We use the [git-flow](https://nvie.com/posts/a-successful-git-branching-model/) branching model. The base branch is `develop`. 99: If you are looking for a stable version, please use the `master` or tags labelled as `v1.x.x`. 100: 101: ## Deployment 102: 103: ### Heroku one-click deploy 104: 105: Deploying Chatwoot to Heroku is a breeze. It's as simple as clicking this button: 106: 107: [![Deploy](https://www.herokucdn.com/deploy/button.svg)](https://heroku.com/deploy?template=https://github.com/chatwoot/chatwoot/tree/master) 108: 109: Follow this [link](https://www.chatwoot.com/docs/environment-variables) to understand setting the correct environment variables for the app to work with all the features. There might be breakages if you do not set the relevant environment variables. 110: 111: 112: ### DigitalOcean 1-Click Kubernetes deployment 113: 114: Chatwoot now supports 1-Click deployment to DigitalOcean as a kubernetes app. 115: 116: 117: 118: 119: 120: ### Other deployment options 121: 122: For other supported options, checkout our [deployment page](https://chatwoot.com/deploy). 123: 124: ## Security 125: 126: Looking to report a vulnerability? Please refer our [SECURITY.md](./SECURITY.md) file. 127: 128: ## Community 129: 130: If you need help or just want to hang out, come, say hi on our [Discord](https://discord.gg/cJXdrwS) server. 131: 132: ## Contributors 133: 134: Thanks goes to all these [wonderful people](https://www.chatwoot.com/docs/contributors): 135: 136: 137: 138: 139: *Chatwoot* © 2017-2026, Chatwoot Inc - Released under the MIT License. 137:   # Be aware: The precision of created_at and last_activity_at may differ from Ruby's Time precision.

260:     return handle_campaign_status if campaign.present?

262:     # TODO: make this an inbox config instead of assuming bot conversations should start as pending
263:     self.status = :pending if inbox.active_bot?

266:   def handle_campaign_status
267:     # If campaign has no sender (bot-initiated) and inbox has active bot, let bot handle it
268:     self.status = :pending if campaign.sender_id.nil? && inbox.active_bot?
269:   end

277: 
278:     Rails.configuration.dispatcher.dispatch(CONVERSATION_DELETED, Time.zone.now, conversation_data: @unread_count_deletion_data)
279:   end

281:   def notify_conversation_updation
282:     return unless previous_changes.keys.present? && allowed_keys?
283: 
284:     dispatch_conversation_updated_event(previous_changes)
285:   end
286: 
287:   def list_of_keys
288:     %w[team_id assignee_id assignee_agent_bot_id status snoozed_until custom_attributes label_list waiting_since
289:        first_reply_created_at priority]
290:   end
291: 
292:   def allowed_keys?
293:     (
294:       previous_changes.keys.intersect?(list_of_keys) ||
295:       (previous_changes['additional_attributes'].present? && previous_changes['additional_attributes'][1].keys.intersect?(%w[conversation_language]))
296:     )
297:   end

299:   def load_attributes_created_by_db_triggers
300:     # Display id is set via a trigger in the database
301:     # So we need to specifically fetch it after the record is created
302:     # We can't use reload because it will clear the previous changes, which we need for the dispatcher
303:     obj_from_db = self.class.find(id)
304:     self[:display_id] = obj_from_db[:display_id]
305:     self[:uuid] = obj_from_db[:uuid]
306:   end
307: 
308:   def notify_status_change
309:     {
310:       CONVERSATION_OPENED => -> { saved_change_to_status? && open? },
311:       CONVERSATION_RESOLVED => -> { saved_change_to_status? && resolved? },
312:       CONVERSATION_STATUS_CHANGED => -> { saved_change_to_status? },
313:       CONVERSATION_READ => -> { saved_change_to_contact_last_seen_at? },
314:       CONVERSATION_CONTACT_CHANGED => -> { saved_change_to_contact_id? }
315:     }.each do |event, condition|
316:       condition.call && dispatcher_dispatch(event, status_change)
317:     end
318:   end
319: 
320:   def dispatcher_dispatch(event_name, changed_attributes = nil)
321:     Rails.configuration.dispatcher.dispatch(event_name, Time.zone.now, conversation: self, notifiable_assignee_change: notifiable_assignee_change?,
322:                                                                        changed_attributes: changed_attributes,
323:                                                                        performed_by: Current.executed_by)
324:   end

326:   def set_unread_count_deletion_data
327:     @unread_count_deletion_data = {
328:       id: id,
329:       account_id: account_id,
330:       inbox_id: inbox_id,
331:       assignee_id: assignee_id,
332:       team_id: team_id,
333:       cached_label_list: cached_label_list
334:     }
335:   end

338:     return false unless open?
339:     # saved_change_to_status? method only works in case of update
340:     return true if previous_changes.key?(:id) || saved_change_to_status?
341:   end

343:   def create_label_change(user_name)
344:     return unless user_name
345: 
346:     previous_labels, current_labels = previous_changes[:label_list]
347:     return unless (previous_labels.is_a? Array) && (current_labels.is_a? Array)
348: 
349:     create_label_added(user_name, current_labels - previous_labels)
350:     create_label_removed(user_name, previous_labels - current_labels)
351:   end

354:     return unless additional_attributes['referer']
355: 
356:     self['additional_attributes']['referer'] = nil unless url_valid?(additional_attributes['referer'])
357:   end

1: class DeleteObjectJob < ApplicationJob
2:   queue_as :low
3: 
4:   BATCH_SIZE = 5_000
5: 
6:   def perform(object, user = nil, ip = nil)
7:     # Pre-purge heavy associations for large objects to avoid
8:     # timeouts & race conditions due to destroy_async fan-out.
9:     purge_heavy_associations(object)
10:     object.destroy!
11:     process_post_deletion_tasks(object, user, ip)
12:   end
13: 
14:   def process_post_deletion_tasks(object, user, ip); end
15: 
16:   private
17: 
18:   def heavy_associations
19:     {
20:       Account => %i[conversations contacts inboxes reporting_events],
21:       Inbox => %i[conversations contact_inboxes reporting_events]
22:     }.freeze
23:   end
24: 
25:   def purge_heavy_associations(object)
26:     klass = heavy_associations.keys.find { |k| object.is_a?(k) }
27:     return unless klass
28: 
29:     heavy_associations[klass].each do |assoc|
30:       next unless object.respond_to?(assoc)
31: 
32:       batch_destroy(object.public_send(assoc))
33:     end
34:   end
35: 
36:   def batch_destroy(relation)
37:     relation.find_in_batches(batch_size: BATCH_SIZE) do |batch|
38:       batch.each(&:destroy!)
39:     end
40:   end
41: end
42: 
43: DeleteObjectJob.prepend_mod_with('DeleteObjectJob')

34:                      elsif channel.google?
35:                        Imap::GoogleFetchEmailService.new(channel: channel, interval: interval).perform
36:                      else

1: class AutoAssignment::AssignmentJob < ApplicationJob
2:   queue_as :default
3: 
4:   IN_FLIGHT_TTL = 5.minutes
5: 
6:   # Coalesce per inbox: at most one AssignmentJob per inbox is in-flight
7:   # (queued or running) at any time. The marker carries a token so a job only
8:   # releases its own claim (a newer job may have taken it after a TTL lapse).
9:   def self.enqueue_for_inbox(inbox_id)
10:     key = format(::Redis::Alfred::AUTO_ASSIGNMENT_IN_FLIGHT_KEY, inbox_id: inbox_id)
11:     token = SecureRandom.uuid
12:     return false unless ::Redis::Alfred.set(key, token, nx: true, ex: IN_FLIGHT_TTL)
13: 
14:     return true if perform_later(inbox_id: inbox_id, token: token)
15: 
16:     # Enqueue was halted; release our own claim so the inbox isn't gated until the TTL.
17:     ::Redis::Alfred.delete_if_equals(key, token)
18:     false
19:   rescue StandardError
20:     # Enqueue raised after we claimed the gate; release our own claim, then re-raise.
21:     ::Redis::Alfred.delete_if_equals(key, token)
22:     raise
23:   end
24: 
25:   def perform(inbox_id:, token: nil)
26:     inbox = Inbox.find_by(id: inbox_id)
27:     return unless inbox
28: 
29:     service = AutoAssignment::AssignmentService.new(inbox: inbox)
30:     assigned_count = service.perform_bulk_assignment(limit: bulk_assignment_limit)
31:     Rails.logger.info "Assigned #{assigned_count} conversations for inbox #{inbox.id}"
32:   rescue StandardError => e
33:     Rails.logger.error "Bulk assignment failed for inbox #{inbox_id}: #{e.message}"
34:     raise e if Rails.env.test?
35:   ensure
36:     release_in_flight(inbox_id, token)
37:   end
38: 
39:   private
40: 
41:   # Release the in-flight marker only if we still own it. The atomic
42:   # compare-and-delete ensures a job whose TTL lapsed can't delete a newer
43:   # job's claim. Tokenless (pre-deploy) jobs never claimed a key, so skip.
44:   def release_in_flight(inbox_id, token)
45:     return if token.nil?
46: 
47:     key = format(::Redis::Alfred::AUTO_ASSIGNMENT_IN_FLIGHT_KEY, inbox_id: inbox_id)
48:     ::Redis::Alfred.delete_if_equals(key, token)
49:   end
50: 
51:   def bulk_assignment_limit
52:     ENV.fetch('AUTO_ASSIGNMENT_BULK_LIMIT', 100).to_i
53:   end
54: end

1: class DeleteObjectJob < ApplicationJob
2:   queue_as :low
3: 
4:   BATCH_SIZE = 5_000
5: 
6:   def perform(object, user = nil, ip = nil)
7:     # Pre-purge heavy associations for large objects to avoid
8:     # timeouts & race conditions due to destroy_async fan-out.
9:     purge_heavy_associations(object)
10:     object.destroy!
11:     process_post_deletion_tasks(object, user, ip)
12:   end
13: 
14:   def process_post_deletion_tasks(object, user, ip); end
15: 
16:   private
17: 
18:   def heavy_associations
19:     {
20:       Account => %i[conversations contacts inboxes reporting_events],
21:       Inbox => %i[conversations contact_inboxes reporting_events]
22:     }.freeze
23:   end
24: 
25:   def purge_heavy_associations(object)
26:     klass = heavy_associations.keys.find { |k| object.is_a?(k) }
27:     return unless klass
28: 
29:     heavy_associations[klass].each do |assoc|
30:       next unless object.respond_to?(assoc)
31: 
32:       batch_destroy(object.public_send(assoc))
33:     end
34:   end
35: 
36:   def batch_destroy(relation)
37:     relation.find_in_batches(batch_size: BATCH_SIZE) do |batch|
38:       batch.each(&:destroy!)
39:     end
40:   end
41: end
